title: Green Vaginal Discharge in Pregnant Cats: Veterinary Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your pregnant cat has green vaginal discharge, it could be a medical emergency. Learn the urgency levels and when to contact your veterinarian.
content_html:
What Is Green Vaginal Discharge?

Green vaginal discharge in a pregnant cat is often caused by the breakdown of hemoglobin into a pigment called uteroverdin. This occurs when a placenta separates from the uterine wall. While this is a natural part of the birthing process once labor has started, its appearance before any kittens are born is a significant clinical sign that requires immediate attention.

Is It an Emergency?

Urgency Level: High. If your cat is showing green discharge but has not yet delivered a kitten, this is considered a veterinary emergency. It typically indicates that at least one kitten's life support system—the placenta—has detached, meaning the kitten is no longer receiving oxygen. Without immediate medical intervention, such as an emergency C-section, the kittens and potentially the mother are at high risk.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

When you contact your emergency veterinarian, providing a clear photo of the discharge and the surrounding area can be life-saving. A photo allows the veterinary team to visually confirm the color, viscosity, and volume of the fluid. This helps them determine if the discharge is a normal sign of impending labor or a critical warning sign of dystocia (difficult birth) or fetal distress, allowing them to prepare the surgical suite before you even arrive.

faqs.question: Is green discharge ever normal during a cat's pregnancy?
faqs.answer: Green discharge is only considered normal if it occurs immediately before the birth of the first kitten or between kittens during active labor. If it appears before labor has started, it is a sign of premature placental separation.

faqs.question: What should I do if I see green discharge and my cat is not straining?
faqs.answer: You should contact an emergency veterinarian immediately. The absence of straining does not mean everything is fine; it may indicate that the cat is in a state of uterine inertia or that the kittens are in distress.

faqs.question: Can green discharge indicate an infection?
faqs.answer: Yes, green or foul-smelling discharge can also be a sign of a uterine infection or a deceased fetus. Both conditions are life-threatening and require urgent veterinary care.
